items.passage: Anglerfish are among the strangest predators living in the dark waters of the deep ocean. A glowing lure dangles above the mouth, and small prey are drawn toward this faint light. The lure shines because tiny bacteria inside it produce a steady chemical glow. Because food is rare at such depths, the anglerfish must wait patiently and strike quickly. Males are tiny and often attach themselves permanently to the much larger female body.

Coral reefs shelter a quarter of all marine species while covering only a tiny fraction of the seafloor. Rising ocean temperatures stress the coral animals and cause them to expel the colorful algae that feed them. This bleaching leaves the reef pale and weakened, and many corals die when warm water lingers. Conservation programs now grow healthy coral fragments in nurseries and transplant them onto damaged reefs. Protecting these fragile habitats also safeguards the fisheries that coastal communities depend upon.

Young children build their understanding of the world through constant play and curious exploration. During the first years, the brain forms new connections at a remarkable pace as experiences accumulate. Around age two, toddlers begin to grasp that objects exist even when they cannot see them. Language then expands rapidly, and children start to combine words into longer meaningful sentences. Researchers believe that warm interaction with caregivers strongly supports this early growth of thinking and memory.

items.subtopic: the invention of the printing press
items.passage: The printing press transformed how knowledge spread across Europe during the fifteenth century. Before this invention, books were copied slowly by hand and remained expensive luxuries for the wealthy. Johannes Gutenberg combined movable metal type with a modified wine press to print pages rapidly. Suddenly identical copies could be produced cheaply, and literacy began to climb among ordinary townspeople. This new technology helped fuel scientific debate, religious reform, and the wider exchange of revolutionary ideas.

Towering mountain ranges arise where two great pieces of the Earth's crust slowly collide. As continental plates push against each other, the rock buckles and folds upward over millions of years. The Himalayas formed when India drifted north and crashed into the vast Asian landmass. Such collisions crumple layers of stone and lift ancient seabeds high above the surrounding plains. Even today these ranges continue to rise, although weathering steadily wears their rugged peaks down.

Gravitational waves are tiny ripples in space itself, predicted long ago by Albert Einstein. These ripples form when enormously heavy objects, such as black holes, spiral together and collide violently. The waves stretch and squeeze space as they travel outward across the universe at light speed. Detecting them requires instruments sensitive enough to measure changes far smaller than a single atom. Their discovery opened a remarkable new window for studying the most dramatic events in distant space.
